The tides are two bulges in the Earth's oceans, one on the side facing the Moon and the other on the side opposite. As the Earth rotates on its axis, one of the ocean bulges (high tide) is held in place "under" the Moon, while another such tide is opposite. The tide under the Moon is explained by the Moon's gravity being stronger on the water close to it. The tide on the opposite side can be explained either by the centrifugal force as the Earth orbits the [barycenter](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") or by the water's inertia as the Moon's gravity is stronger on the solid Earth close to it and it is pulled away from the farther water.[[199]](#cite_note-209)[[200]](#cite_note-210)

Thus, there are two high tides, and two low tides in about 24 hours.[[198]](#cite_note-Lambeck1977-208) Since the Moon is orbiting the Earth in the same direction of the Earth's rotation, the high tides occur about every 12 hours and 25 minutes; the 25 minutes is due to the Moon's time to orbit the Earth.

If the Earth were a water world (one with no continents) it would produce a tide of only one meter, and that tide would be very predictable, but the ocean tides are greatly modified by other effects:

* the frictional coupling of water to Earth's rotation through the ocean floors
* the [inertia](/wiki/Inertia "Inertia") of water's movement
* ocean basins that grow shallower near land
* the sloshing of water between different ocean basins[[201]](#cite_note-211)

As a result, the timing of the tides at most points on the Earth is a product of observations that are explained, incidentally, by theory.

### System evolution

Delays in the tidal peaks of both ocean and solid-body tides cause [torque](/wiki/Torque "Torque") in opposition to the Earth's rotation. This "drains" [angular momentum](/wiki/Angular_momentum "Angular momentum") and rotational [kinetic energy](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") from Earth's rotation, slowing the Earth's rotation.[[198]](#cite_note-Lambeck1977-208)[[195]](#cite_note-touma1994-205) That angular momentum, lost from the Earth, is transferred to the Moon in a process known as [tidal acceleration](/wiki/Tidal_acceleration "Tidal acceleration"), which lifts the Moon into a higher orbit while lowering orbital speed around the Earth. This has resulted in the length of a [anomalistic month](/wiki/Anomalistic_month "Anomalistic month") having increased from 20 days to today's 27.55 days over the course of 3.2 billion years.[[202]](#cite_note-m870-212)

Thus the distance between Earth and Moon is increasing, and the Earth's rotation is slowing in reaction.[[195]](#cite_note-touma1994-205) Measurements from laser reflectors left during the Apollo missions ([lunar ranging experiments](/wiki/Lunar_Laser_Ranging_experiments "Lunar Laser Ranging experiments")) have found that the Moon's distance increases by 38 mm (1.5 in) per year (roughly the rate at which human fingernails grow).[[203]](#cite_note-213)[[204]](#cite_note-214)[[205]](#cite_note-215)
[Atomic clocks](/wiki/Atomic_clock "Atomic clock") show that Earth's Day lengthens by about 17 [microseconds](/wiki/Microsecond "Microsecond") every year,[[206]](#cite_note-216)[[207]](#cite_note-217)[[208]](#cite_note-218) slowly increasing the rate at which [UTC](/wiki/UTC "UTC") is adjusted by [leap seconds](/wiki/Leap_second "Leap second").

This tidal drag makes the rotation of the Earth, and the orbital period of the Moon very slowly match. This matching first results in [tidally locking](/wiki/Tidal_locking "Tidal locking") the lighter body of the orbital system, as is already the case with the Moon. Theoretically, in 50 billion years,[[209]](#cite_note-Universe_Today_2016-219) the Earth's rotation will have slowed to the point of matching the Moon's orbital period, causing the Earth to always present the same side to the Moon. However, the Sun will become a [red giant](/wiki/Red_giant#The_Sun_as_a_red_giant "Red giant"), most likely engulfing the Earth–Moon system long before then.[[210]](#cite_note-220)[[211]](#cite_note-221)

If the Earth–Moon system is not engulfed by the enlarged Sun, the drag from the solar atmosphere can cause the orbit of the Moon to decay. Once the orbit of the Moon closes to a distance of 18,470 km (11,480 mi), it will cross Earth's [Roche limit](/wiki/Roche_limit "Roche limit"), meaning that tidal interaction with Earth would break apart the Moon, turning it into a [ring system](/wiki/Ring_system "Ring system"). Most of the orbiting rings will begin to decay, and the debris will impact Earth. Hence, even if the Sun does not swallow up Earth, the planet may be left moonless.[[212]](#cite_note-space070122-222)

## Orientation and appearance

See also: [Lunar observation](/wiki/Lunar_observation "Lunar observation")

[![Over one lunar month more than half of the Moon's surface can be seen from Earth's surface.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Lunar_libration_with_phase_Oct_2007_HD.gif/250px-Lunar_libration_with_phase_Oct_2007_HD.gif)](/wiki/File:Lunar_libration_with_phase_Oct_2007_HD.gif)

[Libration](/wiki/Libration "Libration"), the slight variation in the Moon's [apparent size](/wiki/Apparent_size "Apparent size") and viewing angle over a single lunar month as viewed from somewhere on the Earth's Northern Hemisphere.

The Moon appears in Earth's sky differently depending on the position of observation on Earth and the time of [lunar year](/wiki/Lunar_year "Lunar year"), [lunar month](/wiki/Lunar_month "Lunar month") and day on Earth. During a year the Moon [culminates](/wiki/Culmination "Culmination") during a day at different [altitudes](/wiki/Altitude_(astronomy) "Altitude (astronomy)"). The Moon appears highest in the sky unlike the Sun during winter and lowest during summer, which is true for the seasons of each of Earth's northern and southern hemispheres.

At the [North](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") and [South Poles](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") the Moon is 24 hours above the horizon for two weeks every [tropical month](/wiki/Tropical_month "Tropical month") (about 27.3 days), comparable to the [polar day](/wiki/Polar_day "Polar day") of the [tropical year](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year"). [Zooplankton](/wiki/Zooplankton "Zooplankton") in the [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") use [moonlight](/wiki/Moonlight "Moonlight") when the Sun is [below the horizon](/wiki/Polar_night "Polar night") for months on end.[[213]](#cite_note-223)

The apparent orientation of the Moon depends on its position in the sky and the hemisphere of the Earth from which it is being viewed. In the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") it appears upside down compared to the view from the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere").[[214]](#cite_note-224) Sometimes the "horns" of a crescent moon appear to be pointing more upwards than sideways. This phenomenon is called a [wet moon](/wiki/Wet_moon "Wet moon") and occurs more frequently in the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics").[[215]](#cite_note-225)

The [distance between the Moon and Earth](/wiki/Lunar_distance "Lunar distance") varies from around 356,400 km (221,500 mi) ([perigee](/wiki/Perigee "Perigee")) to 406,700 km (252,700 mi) (apogee), making the Moon's distance and apparent size fluctuate up to 14%.[[216]](#cite_note-size1-226)[[217]](#cite_note-size2-227) On average the Moon's [angular diameter](/wiki/Angular_diameter "Angular diameter") is about 0.52°, roughly the same apparent size as the Sun (see [§ Eclipses](#Eclipses)). In addition, a purely psychological effect, known as the [Moon illusion](/wiki/Moon_illusion "Moon illusion"), makes the Moon appear larger when close to the horizon.[[218]](#cite_note-228)

### Rotation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Tidal_locking_of_the_Moon_with_the_Earth.gif/250px-Tidal_locking_of_the_Moon_with_the_Earth.gif)](/wiki/File:Tidal_locking_of_the_Moon_with_the_Earth.gif)

Comparison between the Moon on the left, rotating tidally locked (correct), and with the Moon on the right, without rotation (incorrect)

The [tidally locked synchronous rotation](/wiki/Synchronous_rotation "Synchronous rotation") of the Moon as it orbits the Earth results in it always keeping nearly the same face turned towards the planet. The side of the Moon that faces Earth is called the [near side](/wiki/Near_side "Near side"), and the opposite the [far side](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon"). The far side is often inaccurately called the "dark side", but it is in fact illuminated as often as the near side: once every 29.5 Earth days. During [dark moon](/wiki/Dark_moon "Dark moon") to [new moon](/wiki/New_moon "New moon"), the near side is dark.[[219]](#cite_note-229)

The Moon originally rotated at a faster rate, but early in its history its rotation slowed and became [tidally locked](/wiki/Tidally_locked "Tidally locked") in this orientation as a result of [frictional](/wiki/Friction "Friction") effects associated with [tidal](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force") deformations caused by Earth.[[220]](#cite_note-230) With time, the energy of rotation of the Moon on its axis was dissipated as heat, until there was no rotation of the Moon relative to Earth. In 2016, planetary scientists using data collected on the 1998–99 NASA *[Lunar Prospector](/wiki/Lunar_Prospector "Lunar Prospector")* mission found two hydrogen-rich areas (most likely former water ice) on opposite sides of the Moon. It is speculated that these patches were the poles of the Moon billions of years ago before it was tidally locked to Earth.[[221]](#cite_note-231)

### Illumination and phases

See also: [Lunar phase](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase"), [Moonlight](/wiki/Moonlight "Moonlight"), and [Halo (optical phenomenon)](/wiki/Halo_(optical_phenomenon) "Halo (optical phenomenon)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Moon_phases_en.jpg/960px-Moon_phases_en.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moon_phases_en.jpg)

The monthly changes in the angle between the direction of sunlight and view from Earth, and the [phases of the Moon](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase") that result, as viewed from the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"). The [Earth–Moon distance](/wiki/Earth%E2%80%93Moon_distance "Earth–Moon distance") is not to scale.

The Moon rotates, as it orbits Earth, changing orientation toward the Sun, experiencing a [lunar day](/wiki/Lunar_day "Lunar day"). A lunar day is equal to one [lunar month](/wiki/Lunar_month "Lunar month") (one synodic orbit around Earth) due to it being [tidally locked](/wiki/Tidal_locking "Tidal locking") to Earth. Since the Moon is not tidally locked to the Sun, lunar daylight and night times both occur around the Moon. The changing position of the illumination of the Moon by the Sun during a lunar day is observable from Earth as the changing [lunar phases](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase"), waxing crescent being the sunrise and the waning crescent the sunset phase of a day observed from afar.[[222]](#cite_note-r040-232)

Lunar night is the darkest on the far side and during lunar eclipses on the near side (and darker than a moonless night on Earth). During its night, the near side is illuminated by [Earthlight](/wiki/Earthlight "Earthlight") to the extent that lunar surface features may be observable from Earth. Earthshine makes the night on the near side about 43 times brighter, and sometimes even 55 times brighter than a night on Earth illuminated by the light of the full moon.[[223]](#cite_note-u988-233)

In Earth's sky brightness and apparent size of the Moon changes also due to its elliptic [orbit around Earth](/wiki/Orbit_of_the_Moon "Orbit of the Moon"). At [perigee](/wiki/Perigee "Perigee") (closest), since the Moon is up to 14% closer to Earth than at [apogee](/wiki/Apogee "Apogee") (most distant), it subtends a [solid angle](/wiki/Solid_angle "Solid angle") which is up to 30% larger. Consequently, given the same phase, the Moon's brightness also varies by up to 30% between apogee and perigee.[[224]](#cite_note-234) A full (or new) moon at such a position is called a [supermoon](/wiki/Supermoon "Supermoon").[[216]](#cite_note-size1-226)[[217]](#cite_note-size2-227)[[225]](#cite_note-235)

Each of the four intermediate phases of the Moon lasts, on average, about seven days (approximately 7.38 days), but this duration can vary by about ±11% due to the change in the distance between the Moon and Earth between apogee (farthest point) and perigee (closest point).

The number of days since the most recent new moon is called the Moon's age.[[226]](#cite_note-236) A complete cycle of lunar phases is known as a lunation.[[227]](#cite_note-237)

The approximate age and phase of the Moon for any date can be calculated by determining the number of days since a known new moon (e.g., January 1, 1900, or August 11, 1999) and dividing that value by the average length of a synodic month (29.53059 days).[[228]](#cite_note-238) The remainder of the division represents the Moon's age. This method assumes a perfectly circular orbit and does not consider the exact time of the new moon, so results may be off by a few hours. Accuracy decreases as the date moves away from the reference date.

This simplified calculation is suitable for general or decorative purposes, such as moon phase clocks. More precise applications that take into account the Moon's apogee and perigee require more complex methods.

Due to lunar libration, it is sometimes possible to see slightly more than the full moon (up to about 101%) or a small part of its far side (up to 5%).[[229]](#cite_note-239)

#### Observational phenomena

There has been historical controversy over whether observed features on the Moon's surface change over time. Today, many of these claims are thought to be illusory, resulting from observation under different lighting conditions, poor [astronomical seeing](/wiki/Astronomical_seeing "Astronomical seeing"), or inadequate drawings. However, [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing") does occasionally occur and could be responsible for a minor percentage of the reported [lunar transient phenomena](/wiki/Lunar_transient_phenomena "Lunar transient phenomena"). Recently, it has been suggested that a roughly 3 km (1.9 mi) diameter region of the lunar surface was modified by a gas release event about a million years ago.[[230]](#cite_note-240)[[231]](#cite_note-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Two_Lunar_Phases.jpg/250px-Two_Lunar_Phases.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_Lunar_Phases.jpg)

The changing apparent color of the Moon, filtered by Earth's atmosphere

### Albedo and true color of the surface

The Moon's appearance is shaped by its reflective properties and surface composition. Its [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo") is about 0.12,[[232]](#cite_note-242) comparable to worn [asphalt](/wiki/Asphalt_concrete "Asphalt concrete"), making it relatively dark despite its brightness in the night sky. This low albedo represents the reflectance of the lunar regolith, a layer of fine rock fragments created by meteorite impacts. Light scattering by the regolith causes the Moon to appear much brighter at full moon than at quarter phases.

The Moon's true color, without atmospheric effects, is a muted brownish-gray.[[233]](#cite_note-243) This results from silicate minerals in the regolith, with darker basaltic plains (maria) and lighter feldspar-rich highlands. The maria, formed by ancient volcanic flows, contain more iron and titanium, giving them a darker tone. From Earth, the Moon's color can be altered by the atmosphere, appearing red during lunar eclipses or occasionally blue due to volcanic particles.

The Moon also exhibits retro-reflection,[[234]](#cite_note-244) scattering light back toward its source and creating a uniform brightness across its disc without significant limb darkening. Its apparent size and brightness vary due to its elliptical orbit, appearing up to 30% brighter and 14% larger at perigee compared to apogee, a phenomenon known as a "Supermoon".

At times, the Moon can appear red or blue. It may appear red during a [lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse"), because of the red spectrum of the Sun's light being [refracted](/wiki/Refracted "Refracted") onto the Moon by Earth's atmosphere. Because of this red color, lunar eclipses are also sometimes called [blood moons](/wiki/Blood_Moon_(eclipse) "Blood Moon (eclipse)"). The Moon can also seem red when it appears at low angles and through a thick atmosphere.

The Moon may appear blue depending on the presence of certain particles in the air,[[235]](#cite_note-Science_Mission_Directorate_2020-245) such as volcanic particles,[[236]](#cite_note-246) in which case it can be called a [blue moon](/wiki/Blue_moon#Whole_blue_moon "Blue moon").

Because the words "red moon" and "blue moon" can also be used to refer to specific [full moons](/wiki/Full_moon "Full moon") of the year, they do not always refer to the presence of red or blue [moonlight](/wiki/Moonlight "Moonlight").

### Eclipses

Main articles: [Solar eclipse](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse"), [Lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse"), [Solar eclipses on the Moon](/wiki/Solar_eclipses_on_the_Moon "Solar eclipses on the Moon"), and [Eclipse cycle](/wiki/Eclipse_cycle "Eclipse cycle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Solar_eclipse_1999_4.jpg/250px-Solar_eclipse_1999_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solar_eclipse_1999_4.jpg)

A [solar eclipse](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse") causes the Sun to be covered, revealing the white [corona](/wiki/Stellar_corona "Stellar corona").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Full_Eclipse_of_the_Moon_as_seen_in_from_Irvine%2C_CA%2C_USA_%2852075715442%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Full_Eclipse_of_the_Moon_as_seen_in_from_Irvine%2C_CA%2C_USA_%2852075715442%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Full_Eclipse_of_the_Moon_as_seen_in_from_Irvine,_CA,_USA_(52075715442)_(cropped).jpg)

The Moon, tinted reddish, during a [lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse")

Eclipses only occur when the Sun, Earth, and Moon are all in a straight line (termed "[syzygy](/wiki/Syzygy_(astronomy) "Syzygy (astronomy)")"). [Solar eclipses](/wiki/Solar_eclipse "Solar eclipse") occur at [new moon](/wiki/New_moon "New moon"), when the Moon is between the Sun and Earth. In contrast, [lunar eclipses](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") occur at full moon, when Earth is between the Sun and Moon. The apparent size of the Moon is roughly the same as that of the Sun, with both being viewed at close to one-half a degree wide. The Sun is much larger than the Moon, but it is the vastly greater distance that gives it the same apparent size as the much closer and much smaller Moon from the perspective of Earth. The variations in apparent size, due to the non-circular orbits, are nearly the same as well, though occurring in different cycles. This makes possible both [total](/wiki/Total_eclipse "Total eclipse") (with the Moon appearing larger than the Sun) and [annular](/wiki/Annular_eclipse "Annular eclipse") (with the Moon appearing smaller than the Sun) solar eclipses.[[237]](#cite_note-247) In a total eclipse, the Moon completely covers the disc of the Sun and the [solar corona](/wiki/Solar_corona "Solar corona") becomes visible to the [naked eye](/wiki/Naked_eye "Naked eye").

Because the distance between the Moon and Earth is very slowly increasing over time,[[198]](#cite_note-Lambeck1977-208) the angular diameter of the Moon is decreasing. As it evolves toward becoming a [red giant](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant"), the size of the Sun, and its apparent diameter in the sky, are slowly increasing.[[k]](#cite_note-size_changes-248) The combination of these two changes means that hundreds of millions of years ago, the Moon would always completely cover the Sun on solar eclipses, and no annular eclipses were possible. Likewise, hundreds of millions of years in the future, the Moon will no longer cover the Sun completely, and total solar eclipses will not occur.[[238]](#cite_note-fourmilab-249)

As the Moon's orbit around Earth is inclined by about 5.145° (5° 9′) to the [orbit of Earth around the Sun](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic"), eclipses do not occur at every full and new moon. For an eclipse to occur, the Moon must be near the intersection of the two orbital planes.[[239]](#cite_note-eclipse-250) The periodicity and recurrence of eclipses of the Sun by the Moon, and of the Moon by Earth, is described by the [saros](/wiki/Saros_(astronomy) "Saros (astronomy)"), which has a period of approximately 18 years.[[240]](#cite_note-251)

Because the Moon continuously blocks the view of a half-degree-wide circular area of the sky,[[l]](#cite_note-area-252)[[241]](#cite_note-253) the related phenomenon of [occultation](/wiki/Occultation "Occultation") occurs when a bright star or planet passes behind the Moon and is occulted: hidden from view. In this way, a solar eclipse is an occultation of the Sun. Because the Moon is comparatively close to Earth, occultations of individual stars are not visible everywhere on the planet, nor at the same time. Because of the [precession](/wiki/Precession "Precession") of the lunar orbit, each year different stars are occulted.[[242]](#cite_note-254)

### Moon illusion

This section is an excerpt from [Moon illusion](/wiki/Moon_illusion "Moon illusion").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Moon_illusion&action=edit)]

The [Moon illusion](/wiki/Moon_illusion "Moon illusion") is the [optical illusion](/wiki/Optical_illusion "Optical illusion") of the Moon appearing larger near the [horizon](/wiki/Horizon "Horizon") than it does higher up in the [sky](/wiki/Sky "Sky"). It has been known since ancient times and recorded by various cultures.[[243]](#cite_note-Moon_illusion_Wade-255)[[244]](#cite_note-Moon_illusion_Ross_Plug-256)

## History of scientific understanding and exploration

Main articles: [Exploration of the Moon](/wiki/Exploration_of_the_Moon "Exploration of the Moon"), [List of spacecraft that orbited the Moon](/wiki/List_of_spacecraft_that_orbited_the_Moon "List of spacecraft that orbited the Moon"), [List of missions to the Moon](/wiki/List_of_missions_to_the_Moon "List of missions to the Moon"), and [List of lunar probes](/wiki/List_of_lunar_probes "List of lunar probes")

### Pre-telescopic observation (before 1609)

It is believed by some that the oldest [cave paintings](/wiki/Cave_painting "Cave painting") from up to 40,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_present "Before present") of bulls and geometric shapes,[[245]](#cite_note-e093-257) or 20–30,000 year old [tally sticks](/wiki/Tally_stick "Tally stick") were used to observe the phases of the Moon, keeping time using the waxing and waning of [the Moon's phases](/wiki/Lunar_phases "Lunar phases").[[246]](#cite_note-Burton2011-258)
Aspects of the Moon were identified and aggregated in [lunar deities](/wiki/Lunar_deities "Lunar deities") from [prehistoric times](/wiki/Prehistoric_times "Prehistoric times") and were eventually documented and put into symbols from the very first instances of [writing](/wiki/Writing "Writing") in the [4th millennium BC](/wiki/4th_millennium_BC "4th millennium BC"). One of the earliest-discovered possible depictions of the Moon is a 3,000 BCE rock carving *Orthostat 47* at [Knowth](/wiki/Knowth "Knowth"), Ireland.[[247]](#cite_note-Knowth-259)[[248]](#cite_note-spacetoday-260) The [crescent](#Crescent) depicting the Moon as with the lunar deity [Nanna/Sin](/wiki/Sin_(mythology) "Sin (mythology)") have been found from the 3rd millennium BCE.[[249]](#cite_note-BlackGreen1992-261)

The oldest named astronomer and poet [Enheduanna](/wiki/Enheduanna "Enheduanna"), [Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire") high priestess to the lunar deity Nanna/Sin and princess, daughter of [Sargon the Great](/wiki/Sargon_the_Great "Sargon the Great") (c. 2334 – c. 2279 BCE), had the Moon tracked in her chambers.[[250]](#cite_note-c099-262) The oldest found and identified depiction of the Moon in an astronomical relation to other astronomical features is the [Nebra sky disc](/wiki/Nebra_sky_disc "Nebra sky disc") from c. 1800–1600 BCE, depicting features like the [Pleiades](/wiki/Pleiades "Pleiades") next to the Moon.[[251]](#cite_note-g361-263)[[252]](#cite_note-k874-264)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Nebra_solstice_2.jpg/250px-Nebra_solstice_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nebra_solstice_2.jpg)

The [Nebra sky disc](/wiki/Nebra_sky_disc "Nebra sky disc") (c. 1800–1600 BCE), found near a possibly [astronomical complex](/wiki/Astronomical_complex "Astronomical complex"), most likely depicting the Sun or full Moon, the Moon as a crescent, the [Pleiades](/wiki/Pleiades "Pleiades") and the summer and winter solstices as strips of gold on the side of the disc,[[253]](#cite_note-Meller_2021-265)[[254]](#cite_note-266) with the top representing the [horizon](/wiki/Horizon "Horizon")[[255]](#cite_note-:03-267) and [north](/wiki/North "North").

The [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") philosopher [Anaxagoras](/wiki/Anaxagoras "Anaxagoras") (d. 428 BC) reasoned that the Sun and Moon were both giant spherical rocks, and that the latter reflected the light of the former.[[256]](#cite_note-268)[[257]](#cite_note-Needham1986-269): 227 Elsewhere in the 5th century BC to 4th century BC, [Babylonian astronomers](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomers "Babylonian astronomers") had recorded the 18-year [Saros cycle](/wiki/Saros_cycle "Saros cycle") of [lunar eclipses](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse"),[[258]](#cite_note-270) and [Indian astronomers](/wiki/Indian_astronomy "Indian astronomy") had described the Moon's monthly elongation.[[259]](#cite_note-Sarma-Ast-Ind-271) The [Chinese astronomer](/wiki/Chinese_astronomer "Chinese astronomer") [Shi Shen](/wiki/Shi_Shen "Shi Shen") ([fl.](/wiki/Fl. "Fl.") 4th century BC) gave instructions for predicting solar and lunar eclipses.[[257]](#cite_note-Needham1986-269): 411

In [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")'s (384–322 BC) [description of the universe](/wiki/On_the_Heavens "On the Heavens"), the Moon marked the boundary between the spheres of the mutable elements (earth, water, air and fire), and the imperishable stars of [aether](/wiki/Aether_(classical_element) "Aether (classical element)"), an [influential philosophy](/wiki/Aristotelian_physics "Aristotelian physics") that would dominate for centuries.[[260]](#cite_note-272) [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes") (287–212 BC) designed a planetarium that could calculate the motions of the Moon and other objects in the Solar System.[[261]](#cite_note-273) In the 2nd century BC, [Seleucus of Seleucia](/wiki/Seleucus_of_Seleucia "Seleucus of Seleucia") correctly thought that [tides](/wiki/Tide "Tide") were due to the attraction of the Moon, and that their height depends on the Moon's position relative to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun").[[262]](#cite_note-274) In the same century, [Aristarchus](/wiki/On_the_Sizes_and_Distances_(Aristarchus) "On the Sizes and Distances (Aristarchus)") computed the size and distance of the Moon from Earth, obtaining a value of about twenty times the [radius of Earth](/wiki/Radius_of_Earth "Radius of Earth") for the distance.

The Chinese of the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") believed the Moon to be energy equated to *[qi](/wiki/Qi "Qi")* and their 'radiating influence' theory recognized that the light of the Moon was merely a reflection of the Sun; [Jing Fang](/wiki/Jing_Fang "Jing Fang") (78–37 BC) noted the sphericity of the Moon.[[257]](#cite_note-Needham1986-269): 413–414 [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (90–168 AD) greatly improved on the numbers of Aristarchus, calculating a mean distance of 59 times Earth's radius and a diameter of 0.292 Earth diameters, close to the correct values of about 60 and 0.273 respectively.[[263]](#cite_note-275) In the 2nd century AD, [Lucian](/wiki/Lucian "Lucian") wrote the novel *[A True Story](/wiki/A_True_Story "A True Story")*, in which the heroes travel to the Moon and meet its inhabitants. In 510 AD, the Indian astronomer [Aryabhata](/wiki/Aryabhata "Aryabhata") mentioned in his *[Aryabhatiya](/wiki/Aryabhatiya "Aryabhatiya")* that reflected sunlight is the cause of the shining of the Moon.[[264]](#cite_note-Hayashi08Aryabhata-276)[[265]](#cite_note-277) The astronomer and physicist [Ibn al-Haytham](/wiki/Ibn_al-Haytham "Ibn al-Haytham") (965–1039) found that [sunlight](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") was not reflected from the Moon like a mirror, but that light was emitted from every part of the Moon's sunlit surface in all directions.[[266]](#cite_note-278) [Shen Kuo](/wiki/Shen_Kuo "Shen Kuo") (1031–1095) of the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") created an allegory equating the waxing and waning of the Moon to a round ball of reflective silver that, when doused with white powder and viewed from the side, would appear to be a crescent.[[257]](#cite_note-Needham1986-269): 415–416 During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), before the invention of the telescope, the Moon was increasingly recognized as a sphere, though many believed that it was "perfectly smooth".[[267]](#cite_note-279)

### Telescopic exploration (1609–1959)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Galileo%27s_sketches_of_the_moon_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Galileo%27s_sketches_of_the_moon_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Galileo%27s_sketches_of_the_moon_(cropped).png)

The first published sketches of one of the first telescopic observations of the Moon, from the paper *[Sidereus Nuncius](/wiki/Sidereus_Nuncius "Sidereus Nuncius")* (1610) by [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"), providing among other findings the first descriptions of the Moon's topography

The [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope") was developed and reported on in 1608. The first record of telescopic astronomy and rough mapping of the Moon's features is from early summer 1609 by [Thomas Harriot](/wiki/Thomas_Harriot "Thomas Harriot"), but which he did not publish. At the same time [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei") too started to use telescopes to observe the sky and the Moon, recording later that year more detailed observations and crucial conclusions, such as that the Moon was not smooth, featuring mountains and craters, which he published in 1610 in his ground-breaking and soon widely discussed book *[Sidereus Nuncius](/wiki/Sidereus_Nuncius "Sidereus Nuncius")*.

Later in the 17th century, the efforts of [Giovanni Battista Riccioli](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Riccioli "Giovanni Battista Riccioli") and [Francesco Maria Grimaldi](/wiki/Francesco_Maria_Grimaldi "Francesco Maria Grimaldi") led to the system of naming of lunar features in use today. The more exact 1834–1836 *[Mappa Selenographica](/wiki/Mappa_Selenographica "Mappa Selenographica")* of [Wilhelm Beer](/wiki/Wilhelm_Beer "Wilhelm Beer") and [Johann Heinrich von Mädler](/wiki/Johann_Heinrich_von_M%C3%A4dler "Johann Heinrich von Mädler"), and their associated 1837 book *Der Mond*, the first [trigonometrically](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry") accurate study of lunar features, included the heights of more than a thousand mountains, and introduced the study of the Moon at accuracies possible in earthly geography.[[268]](#cite_note-280) Lunar craters, first noted by Galileo, were thought to be [volcanic](/wiki/Volcanic "Volcanic") until the 1870s proposal of [Richard Proctor](/wiki/Richard_Proctor "Richard Proctor") that they were formed by collisions.[[76]](#cite_note-worldbook-84) This view gained support in 1892 from the experimentation of geologist [Grove Karl Gilbert](/wiki/Grove_Karl_Gilbert "Grove Karl Gilbert"), and from comparative studies from 1920 to the 1940s,[[269]](#cite_note-Hall1977-281) leading to the development of [lunar stratigraphy](/wiki/Lunar_geologic_timescale "Lunar geologic timescale"), which by the 1950s was becoming a new and growing branch of [astrogeology](/wiki/Astrogeology "Astrogeology").[[76]](#cite_note-worldbook-84)

### First missions to the Moon (1959–1976)

See also: [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race") and [Moon landing](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing")

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the first [launch systems](/wiki/Launch_system "Launch system") were developed and by the end of the 1950s they reached capabilities that allowed the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to launch [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") into space. The [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") fueled a closely followed development of launch systems by the two states, resulting in the so-called [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race") and its later phase the Moon Race, accelerating efforts and interest in [exploration of the Moon](/wiki/Exploration_of_the_Moon "Exploration of the Moon").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Luna_3_moon.jpg/250px-Luna_3_moon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luna_3_moon.jpg)

First view of the [far side of the Moon](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon"), taken by [Luna 3](/wiki/Luna_3 "Luna 3"), October 7, 1959. Clearly visible is [Mare Moscoviense](/wiki/Mare_Moscoviense "Mare Moscoviense") (top right) and a mare triplet of [Mare Crisium](/wiki/Mare_Crisium "Mare Crisium"), [Mare Marginis](/wiki/Mare_Marginis "Mare Marginis") and [Mare Smythii](/wiki/Mare_Smythii "Mare Smythii") (left center).

After the first spaceflight of [Sputnik 1](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1") in 1957 during [International Geophysical Year](/wiki/International_Geophysical_Year "International Geophysical Year") the spacecraft of the Soviet Union's [*Luna* program](/wiki/Luna_programme "Luna programme") were the first to accomplish a number of goals. Following three unnamed failed missions in 1958,[[270]](#cite_note-282) the first human-made object *[Luna 1](/wiki/Luna_1 "Luna 1")* escaped Earth's gravity and passed the Moon on 4 January 1959. Later that year the first human-made object *[Luna 2](/wiki/Luna_2 "Luna 2")* reached the Moon's surface by [intentionally impacting](/wiki/Lander_(spacecraft)#Impactors "Lander (spacecraft)"). By the end of the year *[Luna 3](/wiki/Luna_3 "Luna 3")* reached as the first human-made object the normally occluded [far side of the Moon](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon"), taking the first photographs of it.
The first spacecraft to perform a successful lunar [soft landing](/wiki/Lander_(spacecraft) "Lander (spacecraft)") was *[Luna 9](/wiki/Luna_9 "Luna 9")* and the first vehicle to orbit the Moon was *[Luna 10](/wiki/Luna_10 "Luna 10")*, both in 1966.[[76]](#cite_note-worldbook-84)

[![The small blue-white semicircle of Earth, almost glowing with color in the blackness of space, rising over the limb of the desolate, cratered surface of the Moon.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/NASA-Apollo8-Dec24-Earthrise.jpg/250px-NASA-Apollo8-Dec24-Earthrise.jpg)](/wiki/File:NASA-Apollo8-Dec24-Earthrise.jpg)

*[Earthrise](/wiki/Earthrise "Earthrise")*, by NASA astronaut [William Anders](/wiki/William_Anders "William Anders"), the first color [image of Earth](/wiki/Timeline_of_first_images_of_Earth_from_space "Timeline of first images of Earth from space") taken by a human from the Moon, during [Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8") (1968), the first time a crewed spacecraft left Earth orbit and reached another [astronomical body](/wiki/Astronomical_body "Astronomical body")

Following President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy")'s 1961 commitment to a crewed Moon landing before the end of the decade, the United States, under NASA leadership, launched a series of uncrewed probes to develop an understanding of the lunar surface in preparation for human missions: the [Jet Propulsion Laboratory](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory")'s [Ranger program](/wiki/Ranger_program "Ranger program"), the [Lunar Orbiter program](/wiki/Lunar_Orbiter_program "Lunar Orbiter program") and the [Surveyor program](/wiki/Surveyor_program "Surveyor program"). The crewed [Apollo program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program") was developed in parallel; after a series of uncrewed and crewed tests of the Apollo spacecraft in Earth orbit, and spurred on by a potential [Soviet lunar human landing](/wiki/Soviet_human_lunar_programs "Soviet human lunar programs"), in 1968 [Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8") made the first human mission to lunar orbit (the first Earthlings, two tortoises, had circled the Moon three months earlier on the Soviet Union's [Zond 5](/wiki/Zond_5 "Zond 5"), followed by turtles on [Zond 6](/wiki/Zond_6 "Zond 6")).

The first time a person landed on the Moon and any extraterrestrial body was when [Neil Armstrong](/wiki/Neil_Armstrong "Neil Armstrong"), the commander of the American mission [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11"), set foot on the Moon at 02:56 UTC on July 21, 1969.[[271]](#cite_note-283) Considered the culmination of the [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race"),[[272]](#cite_note-CNN-284) an estimated 500 million people worldwide watched the transmission by the [Apollo TV camera](/wiki/Apollo_TV_camera "Apollo TV camera"), the largest television audience for a live broadcast at that time.[[273]](#cite_note-285)[[274]](#cite_note-286) While at the same time another mission, the robotic sample return mission [Luna 15](/wiki/Luna_15 "Luna 15") by the Soviet Union had been in orbit around the Moon, becoming together with Apollo 11 the first ever case of two extraterrestrial missions being conducted at the same time.

The Apollo missions 11 to 17 (except [Apollo 13](/wiki/Apollo_13 "Apollo 13"), which aborted its planned lunar landing) removed 380.05 kilograms (837.87 lb) of lunar rock and soil in 2,196 [separate samples](/wiki/Moon_rock "Moon rock").[[275]](#cite_note-287)
Scientific instrument packages were installed on the lunar surface during all the Apollo landings. Long-lived [instrument stations](/wiki/Apollo_Lunar_Surface_Experiments_Package "Apollo Lunar Surface Experiments Package"), including heat flow probes, [seismometers](/wiki/Seismometer "Seismometer"), and [magnetometers](/wiki/Magnetometer "Magnetometer"), were installed at the [Apollo 12](/wiki/Apollo_12 "Apollo 12"), [14](/wiki/Apollo_14 "Apollo 14"), [15](/wiki/Apollo_15 "Apollo 15"), [16](/wiki/Apollo_16 "Apollo 16"), and [17](/wiki/Apollo_17 "Apollo 17") landing sites. Direct transmission of data to Earth concluded in late 1977 because of budgetary considerations,[[276]](#cite_note-288)[[277]](#cite_note-289) but as the stations' [lunar laser ranging](/wiki/Lunar_laser_ranging "Lunar laser ranging") corner-cube retroreflector arrays are passive instruments, they are still being used.[[278]](#cite_note-290)
Apollo 17 in 1972 remains the last crewed mission to the Moon. [Explorer 49](/wiki/Explorer_49 "Explorer 49") in 1973 was the last dedicated U.S. probe to the Moon until 1994.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/AS12-48-7133_%2821470506269%29.jpg/250px-AS12-48-7133_%2821470506269%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AS12-48-7133_(21470506269).jpg)

An example of the robotic and human exploration of the Moon in the first [visit of a separate mission](/wiki/Space_rendezvous "Space rendezvous") beyond [Low Earth Orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_Orbit "Low Earth Orbit") (1969),[[279]](#cite_note-g936-291) [Apollo 12](/wiki/Apollo_12 "Apollo 12") astronaut [Pete Conrad](/wiki/Pete_Conrad "Pete Conrad") checking on [Surveyor 3](/wiki/Surveyor_3 "Surveyor 3") at their shared [lunar base](/wiki/Lunar_base "Lunar base"), with the Apollo 12 *Intrepid* lander and S-band antenna in the distant background

The Soviet Union continued sending robotic missions to the Moon until 1976, deploying in 1970 with [Luna 17](/wiki/Luna_17 "Luna 17") the first remote controlled [rover](/wiki/Rover_(space_exploration) "Rover (space exploration)") [Lunokhod 1](/wiki/Lunokhod_1 "Lunokhod 1") on an extraterrestrial surface, and collecting and returning 0.3 kg of rock and soil samples with three *Luna* [sample return missions](/wiki/Sample_return_mission "Sample return mission") (*[Luna 16](/wiki/Luna_16 "Luna 16")* in 1970, *[Luna 20](/wiki/Luna_20 "Luna 20")* in 1972, and *[Luna 24](/wiki/Luna_24 "Luna 24")* in 1976).[[280]](#cite_note-292)

### Moon Treaty and explorational absence (1976–1990)

Main article: [Moon Treaty](/wiki/Moon_Treaty "Moon Treaty")

Following the [last Soviet mission to the Moon](/wiki/Luna_24 "Luna 24") of 1976, there was little further lunar exploration for fourteen years. Astronautics had shifted its focus towards the exploration of the [inner](/wiki/Inner_Solar_System "Inner Solar System") (e.g. [Venera program](/wiki/Venera_program "Venera program")) and [outer](/wiki/Outer_Solar_System "Outer Solar System") (e.g. [Pioneer 10](/wiki/Pioneer_10 "Pioneer 10"), 1972) [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), but also towards [Earth orbit](/wiki/Geocentric_orbit "Geocentric orbit"), developing and continuously operating, beside [communication satellites](/wiki/Communication_satellite "Communication satellite"), [Earth observation satellites](/wiki/Earth_observation_satellite "Earth observation satellite") (e.g. [Landsat program](/wiki/Landsat_program "Landsat program"), 1972), [space telescopes](/wiki/Space_telescope "Space telescope") and particularly [space stations](/wiki/Space_station "Space station") (e.g. [Salyut program](/wiki/Salyut_program "Salyut program"), 1971).

Negotiation in 1979 of [Moon treaty](/wiki/Moon_treaty "Moon treaty"), and its subsequent ratification in 1984 was the only major activity regarding the Moon until 1990.

### Renewed exploration (1990–present)

In 1990 *[Hiten](/wiki/Hiten_(spacecraft) "Hiten (spacecraft)")* – *Hagoromo*,[[281]](#cite_note-293) the first dedicated lunar mission since 1976, reached the Moon. Sent by Japan, it became the first mission that was not a Soviet Union or U.S. mission to the Moon.

In 1994, the U.S. dedicated a mission to fly a spacecraft (*[Clementine](/wiki/Clementine_(spacecraft) "Clementine (spacecraft)")*) to the Moon again for the first time since 1973. This mission obtained the first near-global topographic map of the Moon, and the first global [multispectral](/wiki/Multispectral "Multispectral") images of the lunar surface.[[282]](#cite_note-294) In 1998, this was followed by the *[Lunar Prospector](/wiki/Lunar_Prospector "Lunar Prospector")* mission, whose instruments indicated the presence of excess hydrogen at the lunar poles, which is likely to have been caused by the presence of water ice in the upper few meters of the regolith within permanently shadowed craters.[[283]](#cite_note-295)

The next years saw a row of first missions to the Moon by a new group of states actively exploring the Moon.
Between 2004 and 2006 the first spacecraft by the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") (ESA) (*[SMART-1](/wiki/SMART-1 "SMART-1")*) reached the Moon, recording the first detailed survey of chemical elements on the lunar surface.[[284]](#cite_note-296)
The [Chinese Lunar Exploration Program](/wiki/Chinese_Lunar_Exploration_Program "Chinese Lunar Exploration Program") reached the Moon for the first time with the orbiter *[Chang'e 1](/wiki/Chang%27e_1 "Chang'e 1")* (2007–2009),[[285]](#cite_note-297) obtaining a full image map of the Moon.
[India](/wiki/Indian_Space_Research_Organisation "Indian Space Research Organisation") reached, orbited and impacted the Moon in 2008 for the first time with its *[Chandrayaan-1](/wiki/Chandrayaan-1 "Chandrayaan-1")* and [Moon Impact Probe](/wiki/Moon_Impact_Probe "Moon Impact Probe"), becoming the fifth and sixth state to do so, creating a high-resolution chemical, mineralogical and photo-geological map of the lunar surface, and confirming the presence of [water molecules in lunar soil](/wiki/Lunar_water "Lunar water").[[286]](#cite_note-298)

The U.S. launched the [*Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter* (LRO)](/wiki/Lunar_Reconnaissance_Orbiter "Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter") and the *[LCROSS](/wiki/LCROSS "LCROSS")* impactor on June 18, 2009. *LCROSS* completed its mission by making a planned and widely observed impact in the crater [Cabeus](/wiki/Cabeus "Cabeus") on October 9, 2009,[[287]](#cite_note-299) whereas *LRO* is currently in operation, obtaining precise lunar [altimetry](/wiki/Altimetry "Altimetry") and high-resolution imagery.

China continued its lunar program in 2010 with *[Chang'e 2](/wiki/Chang%27e_2 "Chang'e 2")*, mapping the surface at a higher resolution over an eight-month period, and in 2013 with *[Chang'e 3](/wiki/Chang%27e_3 "Chang'e 3")*, a lunar [lander](/wiki/Lander_(spacecraft) "Lander (spacecraft)") along with a [lunar rover](/wiki/Lunar_rover "Lunar rover") named *[Yutu](/wiki/Yutu_(rover) "Yutu (rover)")* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 玉兔; lit. 'Jade Rabbit'). This was the first lunar rover mission since *[Lunokhod 2](/wiki/Lunokhod_2 "Lunokhod 2")* in 1973 and the first lunar [soft landing](/wiki/Soft_landing "Soft landing") since *[Luna 24](/wiki/Luna_24 "Luna 24")* in 1976, making China the third country to achieve this.

In 2014 the first privately funded probe, the [Manfred Memorial Moon Mission](/wiki/Manfred_Memorial_Moon_Mission "Manfred Memorial Moon Mission"), flew by the Moon.

Another Chinese rover mission, *[Chang'e 4](/wiki/Chang%27e_4 "Chang'e 4")*, achieved the first landing on [the Moon's far side](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon") in early 2019.[[288]](#cite_note-300)

Also in 2019, India successfully sent its second probe, *[Chandrayaan-2](/wiki/Chandrayaan-2 "Chandrayaan-2")*, to the Moon.

In 2020, China carried out its first robotic [sample return mission](/wiki/Sample_return_mission "Sample return mission") (*[Chang'e 5](/wiki/Chang%27e_5 "Chang'e 5")*), bringing back 1,731 grams of lunar material to Earth.[[289]](#cite_note-301)

The U.S. [developed plans](/wiki/Vision_for_Space_Exploration "Vision for Space Exploration") for returning to the Moon beginning in 2004,[[290]](#cite_note-302) and with the signing of the U.S.-led [Artemis Accords](/wiki/Artemis_Accords "Artemis Accords") in 2020, the [Artemis program](/wiki/Artemis_program "Artemis program") aims to return humans to the Moon in the 2020s.[[291]](#cite_note-303) The Accords have been joined by a growing number of countries. The introduction of the Artemis Accords has fueled a renewed discussion about the international framework and cooperation of lunar activity, building on the [Moon Treaty](/wiki/Moon_Treaty "Moon Treaty") and the ESA-led [Moon Village](/wiki/Moon_Village "Moon Village") concept.[[292]](#cite_note-The_Space_Review_2020-304)[[293]](#cite_note-Australian_Institute_of_International_Affairs_2021-305)[[294]](#cite_note-The_Space_Treaty_Institute_–_Dedicated_to_Peace_and_Sustainability_in_Outer_Space._Our_Mission-306)

2022 South Korea launched [Danuri](/wiki/Danuri "Danuri") successfully, its first mission to the Moon, from the US.
In 2023 and 2024 India and Japan became the fourth and fifth country to [soft land](/wiki/Soft_landing "Soft landing") a spacecraft on the Moon, following the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and United States in the 1960s, and China in the 2010s.[[295]](#cite_note-307) Notably, Japan's spacecraft, the [Smart Lander for Investigating Moon](/wiki/Smart_Lander_for_Investigating_Moon "Smart Lander for Investigating Moon"), survived 3 lunar nights.[[296]](#cite_note-308) In 2024, the [IM-1](/wiki/IM-1 "IM-1") lander became the first commercially built lander to land on the Moon.[[297]](#cite_note-309)

China launched the [Chang'e 6](/wiki/Chang%27e_6 "Chang'e 6") on May 3, 2024, which conducted another lunar sample return from the [far side of the Moon](/wiki/Far_side_of_the_Moon "Far side of the Moon").[[298]](#cite_note-AJ_FI-20230425-310) It also carried a Chinese rover to conduct [infrared spectroscopy](/wiki/Absorption_spectroscopy "Absorption spectroscopy") of lunar surface.[[299]](#cite_note-311) [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") sent a lunar orbiter called [ICUBE-Q](/wiki/ICUBE-Q "ICUBE-Q") along with Chang'e 6.[[300]](#cite_note-312)

[Nova-C 2](/wiki/IM-2 "IM-2"), [iSpace Lander](/wiki/Ispace_(Japanese_company)#Hakuto-R_program "Ispace (Japanese company)") and [Blue Ghost](/wiki/Blue_Ghost "Blue Ghost") were all launched to the Moon in 2024.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Artemis_2_Crew_Portrait.jpg/250px-Artemis_2_Crew_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artemis_2_Crew_Portrait.jpg)

[Artemis II](/wiki/Artemis_II "Artemis II") crew, with the [first woman](/wiki/Women_in_space "Women in space"), person of color and non–US-citizen astronaut to fly around the Moon, scheduled for 2026, returning humans to cislunar space for the first time since [Apollo 17](/wiki/Apollo_17 "Apollo 17") in 1972. [Clockwise](/wiki/Clockwise "Clockwise") from left: [Koch](/wiki/Christina_Koch "Christina Koch"), [Glover](/wiki/Victor_J._Glover "Victor J. Glover"), [Hansen](/wiki/Jeremy_Hansen "Jeremy Hansen") and [Wiseman](/wiki/Reid_Wiseman "Reid Wiseman").

NASA plans to launch its [Artemis II](/wiki/Artemis_II "Artemis II") crewed lunar flyby in April 2026, becoming the first crewed lunar mission since the Apollo era. Crew members [Christina Koch](/wiki/Christina_Koch "Christina Koch"), [Victor Glover](/wiki/Victor_Glover_(astronaut) "Victor Glover (astronaut)"), and [Jeremy Hansen](/wiki/Jeremy_Hansen "Jeremy Hansen") will become the first woman, person of color, and non-US citizen to reach deep space and the Moon's vicinity.[[301]](#cite_note-313) Following Artemis II, NASA plans to return humans to the Moon with the [Artemis IV](/wiki/Artemis_IV "Artemis IV") landing on the lunar South Pole in 2028.[[302]](#cite_note-314)

### Future

See also: [List of proposed missions to the Moon](/wiki/List_of_proposed_missions_to_the_Moon "List of proposed missions to the Moon")

Beside the progressing [Artemis program](/wiki/Artemis_program "Artemis program") and supporting [Commercial Lunar Payload Services](/wiki/Commercial_Lunar_Payload_Services "Commercial Lunar Payload Services"),[[303]](#cite_note-315) China is continuing its ambitious [Chang'e program](/wiki/Chang%27e_program "Chang'e program"), having announced with Russia's struggling *[Luna-Glob](/wiki/Luna-Glob "Luna-Glob")* program joint missions.[[304]](#cite_note-TASS_2019_x202-316)[[305]](#cite_note-317) Both the Chinese and US lunar programs have the goal to establish in the 2030s a [lunar base](/wiki/Lunar_base "Lunar base") with their international partners, though the US and its partners will first establish an orbital [Lunar Gateway](/wiki/Lunar_Gateway "Lunar Gateway") station in the 2020s, from which Artemis missions will land the [Human Landing System](/wiki/Human_Landing_System "Human Landing System") to set up temporary surface camps.

While the Apollo missions were explorational in nature, the Artemis program plans to establish a more permanent presence. To this end, NASA is partnering with industry leaders to establish key elements such as modern communication infrastructure. A [4G](/wiki/4G "4G") connectivity demonstration is to be launched aboard an [Intuitive Machines Nova-C](/wiki/Intuitive_Machines_Nova-C "Intuitive Machines Nova-C") lander in 2024.[[306]](#cite_note-318) Another focus is on [in situ resource utilization](/wiki/In_situ_resource_utilization "In situ resource utilization"), which is a key part of the [DARPA lunar programs](/wiki/DARPA_lunar_programs "DARPA lunar programs"). [DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA") has requested that industry partners develop a 10–year lunar architecture plan to enable the beginning of a lunar economy.[[307]](#cite_note-319)

## Human interaction and presence

See also: [Human presence in space](/wiki/Human_presence_in_space "Human presence in space")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Moon_Soft_Landings.svg/250px-Moon_Soft_Landings.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Moon_Soft_Landings.svg)

Map of all the sites of [soft landings](/wiki/Soft_landing "Soft landing") on the Moon (2024)

In 1959 the first extraterrestrial probes reached the Moon ([Luna program](/wiki/Luna_program "Luna program")), just a year into the [space age](/wiki/Space_age "Space age"), after the first ever orbital flight. Since then, humans have sent a range of probes and people to the Moon. The first stay of people on the Moon was conducted in 1969, in a series of crewed exploration missions (the [Apollo Program](/wiki/Apollo_Program "Apollo Program")), the last having taken place in 1972.

Uninterrupted presence has been the case through the [remains](/wiki/List_of_artificial_objects_on_the_Moon "List of artificial objects on the Moon") of impactors, landings and [lunar orbiters](/wiki/List_of_extraterrestrial_orbiters#Moon "List of extraterrestrial orbiters"). Some landings and orbiters have maintained a small lunar infrastructure, providing continuous observation and communication at the Moon. Longterm missions continuing to be active are some orbiters such as the 2009-launched [Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter](/wiki/Lunar_Reconnaissance_Orbiter "Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter") surveilling the Moon for future missions, as well as some Landers such as the 2013-launched [Chang'e 3](/wiki/Chang%27e_3 "Chang'e 3") with its Lunar Ultraviolet Telescope still operational.[[308]](#cite_note-320)
Five retroreflectors have been installed on the Moon since the 1970s and since used for accurate measurements of the physical [librations](/wiki/Libration "Libration") through [laser ranging to the Moon](/wiki/Lunar_Laser_Ranging_experiment "Lunar Laser Ranging experiment").

Increasing human activity in [cislunar space](/wiki/Cislunar_space "Cislunar space") as well as on the Moon's surface, particularly missions at the far side of the Moon or the [lunar north](/wiki/Lunar_north_pole "Lunar north pole") and [south polar](/wiki/Lunar_south_pole "Lunar south pole") regions, are in need for a lunar infrastructure. For that purpose, orbiters in [orbits around the Moon](/wiki/Lunar_orbit "Lunar orbit") or [the Earth–Moon Lagrange points](/wiki/List_of_objects_at_Lagrange_points#Earth–Moon_Lagrange_points "List of objects at Lagrange points"), have since 2006 been operated. With highly [eccentric orbits](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") providing continuous communication, as with the orbit of [Queqiao](/wiki/Queqiao "Queqiao") and [Queqiao-2 relay satellite](/wiki/Queqiao-2_relay_satellite "Queqiao-2 relay satellite") or the planned first extraterrestrial space station, the [Lunar Gateway](/wiki/Lunar_Gateway "Lunar Gateway").[[309]](#cite_note-Williams_2022-321)[[310]](#cite_note-Phys.org_2021-322)

There are [several missions by different agencies and companies planned](/wiki/List_of_missions_to_the_Moon#Future_missions "List of missions to the Moon") to establish a long-term human presence on the Moon, with the [Lunar Gateway](/wiki/Lunar_Gateway "Lunar Gateway") as the currently most advanced project as part of the [Artemis program](/wiki/Artemis_program "Artemis program").

### Human impact

See also: [Space debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris"), [Space sustainability](/wiki/Space_sustainability "Space sustainability"), [List of artificial objects on the Moon](/wiki/List_of_artificial_objects_on_the_Moon "List of artificial objects on the Moon"), [Space art § Art in space](/wiki/Space_art#Art_in_space "Space art"), [Moonbase](/wiki/Moonbase "Moonbase"), [Lunar resources § Mining](/wiki/Lunar_resources#Mining "Lunar resources"), [Tourism on the Moon](/wiki/Tourism_on_the_Moon "Tourism on the Moon"), and [Space archaeology](/wiki/Space_archaeology "Space archaeology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/ALSEP_AS17-134-20500.jpg/250px-ALSEP_AS17-134-20500.jpg)](/wiki/File:ALSEP_AS17-134-20500.jpg)

Artifacts of human activity, [Apollo 17's Lunar Surface Experiments Package](/wiki/Apollo_Lunar_Surface_Experiments_Package "Apollo Lunar Surface Experiments Package")[[311]](#cite_note-Garber_2012-323)

While the Moon has the lowest [planetary protection target-categorization](/wiki/Planetary_protection#Target_categories "Planetary protection"), its degradation as a pristine body and scientific place has been discussed.[[312]](#cite_note-Vidaurri_2019-324) If there is [astronomy performed from the Moon](#Astronomy_from_the_Moon), it will need to be free from any physical and [radio pollution](/wiki/Radio_pollution "Radio pollution"). While the Moon has no significant atmosphere, traffic and impacts on the Moon causes clouds of dust that can spread far and possibly contaminate the original state of the Moon and its special scientific content.[[313]](#cite_note-David_2020-325) Scholar [Alice Gorman](/wiki/Alice_Gorman "Alice Gorman") asserts that, although the Moon is inhospitable, describing the Moon as dead negates its dynamics, which requires sustainable human activity to treat the Moon's ecology as a co-participant.[[314]](#cite_note-Gorman_2022-326)

The so-called "[Tardigrade affair](/wiki/Tardigrades_on_the_Moon "Tardigrades on the Moon")" of the 2019 crashed [Beresheet](/wiki/Beresheet "Beresheet") lander and its carrying of [tardigrades](/wiki/Tardigrade "Tardigrade") has been discussed as an example for lacking measures and lacking international regulation for [planetary protection](/wiki/Planetary_protection "Planetary protection").[[315]](#cite_note-Alvarez_2020_p.-327)

[Space debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris") beyond Earth around the Moon has been considered as a future challenge with increasing numbers of missions to the Moon, particularly as a danger for such missions.[[316]](#cite_note-Carter_2022-328)[[317]](#cite_note-Nast_2013-329) As such lunar waste management has been raised as an issue which future lunar missions, particularly on the surface, need to tackle.[[318]](#cite_note-Pino_Salmeri_Hugo_Hume_p.-330)[[319]](#cite_note-NASA/ADS-331)

Human remains have been transported to the Moon, including by private companies such as [Celestis](/wiki/Celestis "Celestis") and [Elysium Space](/wiki/Elysium_Space "Elysium Space"). Because the Moon has been sacred or significant to many cultures, the practice of [space burials](/wiki/Space_burial "Space burial") have attracted criticism from [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") leaders. For example, then–[Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") president [Albert Hale](/wiki/Albert_Hale "Albert Hale") criticized NASA for sending the [cremated](/wiki/Cremated "Cremated") ashes of scientist [Eugene Shoemaker](/wiki/Eugene_Shoemaker "Eugene Shoemaker") to the Moon in 1998.[[320]](#cite_note-Magazine_Sullivan_2024_a329-332)[[321]](#cite_note-web.archive.org_2011_c901-333)

Beside the remains of human activity on the Moon, there have been some intended permanent installations like the *[Moon Museum](/wiki/Moon_Museum "Moon Museum")* art piece, [Apollo 11 goodwill messages](/wiki/Apollo_11_goodwill_messages "Apollo 11 goodwill messages"), six [lunar plaques](/wiki/Lunar_plaque "Lunar plaque"), the *[Fallen Astronaut](/wiki/Fallen_Astronaut "Fallen Astronaut")* memorial, and other artifacts.[[311]](#cite_note-Garber_2012-323)

### Astronomy from the Moon

Further information: [Extraterrestrial sky § The Moon](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_sky#The_Moon "Extraterrestrial sky")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Earth%E2%80%99s_geocorona_from_the_Moon.jpg/250px-Earth%E2%80%99s_geocorona_from_the_Moon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Earth%E2%80%99s_geocorona_from_the_Moon.jpg)

Earth's [exosphere](/wiki/Exosphere "Exosphere") illuminated creating its [geocorona](/wiki/Geocorona "Geocorona"), visible in ultraviolet and viewed by the [Far Ultraviolet Camera/Spectrograph](/wiki/Far_Ultraviolet_Camera/Spectrograph "Far Ultraviolet Camera/Spectrograph") of [Apollo 16](/wiki/Apollo_16 "Apollo 16") in 1972 from the Moon's surface

The Moon has been used as a site for astronomical and [Earth observations](/wiki/Earth_observation "Earth observation"). The Earth appears in the [Moon's sky](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_sky#The_Moon "Extraterrestrial sky") with an [apparent size](/wiki/Apparent_size "Apparent size") of 1° 48′ to 2°,[[322]](#cite_note-Gorkavyi_Krotkov_Marshak_2023_pp._1527–1537-334) three to four times the size of the Moon or Sun in Earth's sky, or about the apparent width of two little fingers at an arm's length away. Observations from the Moon started as early as 1966 with the [first images of Earth](/wiki/Timeline_of_first_images_of_Earth_from_space "Timeline of first images of Earth from space") from the Moon, taken by [Lunar Orbiter 1](/wiki/Lunar_Orbiter_1 "Lunar Orbiter 1"). Of particular cultural significance is the 1968 photograph called *[Earthrise](/wiki/Earthrise "Earthrise")*, taken by [Bill Anders](/wiki/Bill_Anders "Bill Anders") of [Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8") in 1968. In April 1972 the [Apollo 16](/wiki/Apollo_16 "Apollo 16") mission set up the first dedicated telescope,[[323]](#cite_note-w422-335)[[324]](#cite_note-x909-336) the [Far Ultraviolet Camera/Spectrograph](/wiki/Far_Ultraviolet_Camera/Spectrograph "Far Ultraviolet Camera/Spectrograph"), recording various astronomical photos and spectra.[[325]](#cite_note-337)

The Moon is recognized as an excellent site for telescopes.[[326]](#cite_note-338) It is relatively nearby; certain craters near the poles are permanently dark and cold and especially useful for [infrared telescopes](/wiki/Infrared_telescope "Infrared telescope"); and [radio telescopes](/wiki/Radio_telescope "Radio telescope") on the far side would be shielded from the radio chatter of Earth.[[327]](#cite_note-339) The [lunar soil](/wiki/Lunar_soil "Lunar soil"), although it poses a problem for any moving parts of [telescopes](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope"), can be mixed with [carbon nanotubes](/wiki/Carbon_nanotube "Carbon nanotube") and [epoxies](/wiki/Epoxy "Epoxy") and employed in the construction of mirrors up to 50 meters in diameter.[[328]](#cite_note-340) A lunar [zenith telescope](/wiki/Zenith_telescope "Zenith telescope") can be made cheaply with an [ionic liquid](/wiki/Ionic_liquid "Ionic liquid").[[329]](#cite_note-341)

With increased human activity on the surface of the Moon the atmospheric circulation of lunar dust will increase, reducing the favourable conditions for astronomy from the surface depending on measures to mitigate the spread of lunar dust.[[330]](#cite_note-y615-342)

### Living on the Moon

Main article: [Lunar habitation](/wiki/Lunar_habitation "Lunar habitation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Aldrin_Looks_Back_at_Tranquility_Base_-_GPN-2000-001102.jpg/250px-Aldrin_Looks_Back_at_Tranquility_Base_-_GPN-2000-001102.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aldrin_Looks_Back_at_Tranquility_Base_-_GPN-2000-001102.jpg)

Astronaut [Buzz Aldrin](/wiki/Buzz_Aldrin "Buzz Aldrin") in [life-supporting suit](/wiki/Apollo/Skylab_spacesuit "Apollo/Skylab spacesuit") looking back at the first [lunar habitat and base](/wiki/Lunar_base "Lunar base"), the [Lunar Module *Eagle*](/wiki/Lunar_Module_Eagle "Lunar Module Eagle") of [Tranquility Base](/wiki/Tranquility_Base "Tranquility Base"), during [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") (1969), the first crewed [Moon landing](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing")

Humans have lived on the Moon in groups of two and for up to three days.[[331]](#cite_note-i632-343) Altogether twelve people have stayed on the Moon over the course of six visits.[[332]](#cite_note-n138-344) They all lived in one type of [surface habitat](/wiki/Space_habitat_(facility)#Extraterrestrial_surface_habitat "Space habitat (facility)"), the [Apollo Lunar Module](/wiki/Apollo_Lunar_Module "Apollo Lunar Module").[[333]](#cite_note-345) In the course of these stays some people have spent in total up to one day roaming the surface.[[331]](#cite_note-i632-343)

Challenges when roaming the surface arise from [lunar dust](/wiki/Lunar_dust "Lunar dust") sticking to suits and tools, and has been carried inside the habitats. Astronauts could taste and smell the dust, which smells like gunpowder and was called the "Apollo aroma".[[334]](#cite_note-Aroma-346) This fine lunar dust can [cause health issues](/wiki/Lunar_dust#Harmful_effects_of_lunar_dust "Lunar dust").[[334]](#cite_note-Aroma-346)

In 2019, at least one plant seed sprouted in an experiment on the [Chang'e 4 lander](/wiki/Change_4#Lunar_lander "Change 4"). It was carried from Earth along with other small life in its *Lunar Micro Ecosystem*.[[335]](#cite_note-Seeds-347)

### Legal status

See also: [Space law](/wiki/Space_law "Space law"), [Politics of outer space](/wiki/Politics_of_outer_space "Politics of outer space"), [Space advocacy](/wiki/Space_advocacy "Space advocacy"), [Colonization of the Moon](/wiki/Colonization_of_the_Moon "Colonization of the Moon"), [Outer Space Treaty](/wiki/Outer_Space_Treaty "Outer Space Treaty"), and [Moon Treaty](/wiki/Moon_Treaty "Moon Treaty")

Although *[Luna](/wiki/Luna_program "Luna program")* landers scattered pennants of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") on the Moon, and [U.S. flags](/wiki/Lunar_Flag_Assembly "Lunar Flag Assembly") were symbolically planted at their landing sites by the [Apollo astronauts](/wiki/Apollo_astronauts "Apollo astronauts"), no nation claims ownership of any part of the Moon's surface.[[336]](#cite_note-unoosa_q6-348) Likewise no [private ownership of parts of the Moon, or as a whole](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_real_estate "Extraterrestrial real estate"), is considered credible.[[337]](#cite_note-unoosa_q7-349)[[338]](#cite_note-iisl_2004-350)[[339]](#cite_note-iisl_2009-351)

The 1967 [Outer Space Treaty](/wiki/Outer_Space_Treaty "Outer Space Treaty") defines the Moon and all outer space as the "[province of all mankind](/wiki/Common_heritage_of_mankind "Common heritage of mankind")".[[336]](#cite_note-unoosa_q6-348) It restricts the use of the Moon to peaceful purposes, explicitly banning military installations and [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction").[[340]](#cite_note-unoosa_q5-352) A majority of countries are parties of this treaty.[[341]](#cite_note-unoosa_q4-353)
The 1979 [Moon Agreement](/wiki/Moon_Agreement "Moon Agreement") was created to elaborate, and restrict the exploitation of the [Moon's resources](/wiki/Lunar_resources "Lunar resources") by any single nation, leaving it to a yet unspecified international regulatory regime.[[342]](#cite_note-The_Space_Review_2021-354) As of January 2020, it has been signed and ratified by 18 nations,[[343]](#cite_note-unoosa_moon-355) none of which have [human spaceflight](/wiki/Human_spaceflight "Human spaceflight") capabilities.

Since 2020, countries have joined the U.S. in their [Artemis Accords](/wiki/Artemis_Accords "Artemis Accords"), which are challenging the treaty. The U.S. has furthermore emphasized in a presidential [executive order](/wiki/Executive_order "Executive order") ("Encouraging International Support for the Recovery and Use of Space Resources.") that "the United States does not view outer space as a 'global commons'" and calls the Moon Agreement "a failed attempt at constraining free enterprise".[[344]](#cite_note-356)[[345]](#cite_note-357)

With Australia signing and ratifying both the Moon Treaty in 1986 as well as the Artemis Accords in 2020, there has been a discussion if they can be harmonized.[[293]](#cite_note-Australian_Institute_of_International_Affairs_2021-305) In this light an *Implementation Agreement* for the Moon Treaty has been advocated for, as a way to compensate for the shortcomings of the Moon Treaty and to harmonize it with other laws and agreements such as the Artemis Accords, allowing it to be more widely accepted.[[292]](#cite_note-The_Space_Review_2020-304)[[294]](#cite_note-The_Space_Treaty_Institute_–_Dedicated_to_Peace_and_Sustainability_in_Outer_Space._Our_Mission-306)

In the face of such increasing commercial and national interest, particularly prospecting territories, U.S. lawmakers have introduced in late 2020 specific regulation for the conservation of historic landing sites[[346]](#cite_note-HowStuffWorks_2021-358) and interest groups have argued for making such sites [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")[[347]](#cite_note-For_All_Moonkind-359) and zones of scientific value protected zones, all of which add to the legal availability and territorialization of the Moon.[[315]](#cite_note-Alvarez_2020_p.-327)

In 2021, the *Declaration of the Rights of the Moon*[[348]](#cite_note-Declaration-360) was created by a group of "lawyers, space archaeologists and concerned citizens", drawing on precedents in the [Rights of Nature](/wiki/Rights_of_Nature "Rights of Nature") movement and the concept of legal personality for non-human entities in space.[[349]](#cite_note-361)[[350]](#cite_note-Evans_2021-362)

#### Coordination and regulation

Increasing human activity at the Moon has raised the need for coordination to safeguard international and commercial lunar activity. Issues from cooperation to mere coordination, through for example the development of a shared [Lunar time](/wiki/Lunar_time "Lunar time"), have been raised.

In particular the establishment of an [international](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)") or [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") regulatory regime for lunar human activity has been called for by the [Moon Treaty](/wiki/Moon_Treaty "Moon Treaty") and suggested through an *Implementation Agreement*,[[292]](#cite_note-The_Space_Review_2020-304)[[294]](#cite_note-The_Space_Treaty_Institute_–_Dedicated_to_Peace_and_Sustainability_in_Outer_Space._Our_Mission-306) but remains contentious. Current lunar programs are [multilateral](/wiki/Multilateralism "Multilateralism"), with the US-led [Artemis program](/wiki/Artemis_program "Artemis program") and the China-led [International Lunar Research Station](/wiki/International_Lunar_Research_Station "International Lunar Research Station"). For broader international cooperation and coordination, the [International Lunar Exploration Working Group](/wiki/International_Lunar_Exploration_Working_Group "International Lunar Exploration Working Group") (ILEWG), the [Moon Village Association](/wiki/Moon_Village "Moon Village") (MVA) and more generally the International Space Exploration Coordination Group (ISECG) has been established.

## In culture and life

### Timekeeping

Further information: [Lunar calendar](/wiki/Lunar_calendar "Lunar calendar"), [Lunisolar calendar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar"), and [Metonic cycle](/wiki/Metonic_cycle "Metonic cycle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Venus-de-Laussel-detail-corne.jpg/250px-Venus-de-Laussel-detail-corne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Venus-de-Laussel-detail-corne.jpg)

The [Venus of Laussel](/wiki/Venus_of_Laussel "Venus of Laussel") (c. 25,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_present "Before present")) holding a crescent shaped horn. The 13 notches on the horn may symbolize the average number of days from menstruation to an [ovulation](/wiki/Ovulation "Ovulation"), or the approximate number of full [menstrual cycles](/wiki/Menstrual_cycle "Menstrual cycle") and [lunar cycles](/wiki/Lunar_cycle "Lunar cycle") per year (although these two phenomena are unrelated).[[351]](#cite_note-:0b-363)[[352]](#cite_note-Boyle_2019-364)

Since pre-historic times people have taken note of [the Moon's phases](/wiki/Lunar_phases "Lunar phases") and its [waxing and waning cycle](/wiki/Lunar_cycle "Lunar cycle") and used it to keep record of time. [Tally sticks](/wiki/Tally_stick "Tally stick"), notched bones dating as far back as 20–30,000 years ago, are believed by some to mark the phases of the Moon.[[246]](#cite_note-Burton2011-258)[[353]](#cite_note-365)[[354]](#cite_note-366) The counting of the days between the Moon's phases eventually gave rise to generalized [time periods](/wiki/Unit_of_time "Unit of time") of lunar cycles as [months](/wiki/Month "Month"), and possibly of its phases as [weeks](/wiki/Week "Week").[[355]](#cite_note-Zerubavel_1989_p._9-367)

The words for the month in a range of different languages carry this relation between the period of the month and the Moon etymologically. The English *month* as well as *moon*, and its cognates in other Indo-European languages (e.g. the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *mensis* and [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") μείς (*meis*) or μήν (mēn), meaning "month")[[356]](#cite_note-368)[[357]](#cite_note-369)[[358]](#cite_note-370)[[359]](#cite_note-371) stem from the [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European "Proto-Indo-European") (PIE) root of *moon*, \**méh1nōt*, derived from the PIE verbal root \**meh1*-, "to measure", "indicat[ing] a functional conception of the Moon, i.e. marker of the month" ([cf.](/wiki/Cf. "Cf.") the English words *measure* and *menstrual*).[[360]](#cite_note-372)[[361]](#cite_note-373)[[362]](#cite_note-374) To give another example from a different [language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family"), the [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") uses the same word ([月](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9C%88 "wikt:月")) for *moon* as for *month*, which furthermore can be found in the symbols for the word *week* ([星期](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%98%9F%E6%9C%9F "wikt:星期")).

This lunar timekeeping gave rise to the historically dominant, but varied, [lunisolar calendars](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar"). The 7th-century [Islamic calendar](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar") is an example of a purely [lunar calendar](/wiki/Lunar_calendar "Lunar calendar"), where months are traditionally determined by the visual sighting of the hilal, or earliest crescent moon, over the horizon.[[363]](#cite_note-375)

Of particular significance has been the occasion of [full moon](/wiki/Full_moon "Full moon"), highlighted and celebrated in a range of calendars and cultures, an example being the Buddhist [Vesak](/wiki/Vesak "Vesak"). The full moon around the [southern](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox") or [northern autumnal equinox](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox") is often called the [harvest moon](/wiki/Harvest_moon "Harvest moon") and is celebrated with festivities such as the [Harvest Moon Festival](/wiki/Harvest_Moon_Festival "Harvest Moon Festival") of the [Chinese lunar calendar](/wiki/Chinese_lunar_calendar "Chinese lunar calendar"), its second most important celebration after [the Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") lunisolar [Lunar New Year](/wiki/Lunar_New_Year "Lunar New Year").[[364]](#cite_note-Confucius_Institute_for_Scotland_2022-376)

Furthermore, association of time with the Moon can also be found in religion, such as the ancient Egyptian temporal and lunar deity [Khonsu](/wiki/Khonsu "Khonsu").

### Cultural representation

Further information: [Cultural astronomy](/wiki/Cultural_astronomy "Cultural astronomy"), [Archaeoastronomy](/wiki/Archaeoastronomy "Archaeoastronomy"), [Lunar deity](/wiki/Lunar_deity "Lunar deity"), [Selene](/wiki/Selene "Selene"), [Luna (goddess)](/wiki/Luna_(goddess) "Luna (goddess)"), [Crescent](/wiki/Crescent "Crescent"), and [Man in the Moon](/wiki/Man_in_the_Moon "Man in the Moon")

See also: [Nocturne (painting)](/wiki/Nocturne_(painting) "Nocturne (painting)") and [Moon magic](/wiki/Moon_magic "Moon magic")

Recurring lunar [aspects](/wiki/Aspect_(religion) "Aspect (religion)") of [lunar deities](/wiki/Lunar_deities "Lunar deities")

[![Sumerian cylinder seal and impression, dated c. 2100 BC, of Ḫašḫamer, ensi (governor) of Iškun-Sin c. 2100 BC. The seated figure is probably king Ur-Nammu, bestowing the governorship on Ḫašḫamer, who is led before him by Lamma (protective goddess).[365]](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Sumerian_Cylinder_Seal_of_King_Ur-Nammu.jpg/250px-Sumerian_Cylinder_Seal_of_King_Ur-Nammu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sumerian_Cylinder_Seal_of_King_Ur-Nammu.jpg)

The [crescent](/wiki/Crescent "Crescent") of [Nanna/Sîn](/wiki/Sin_(mythology) "Sin (mythology)"), c. 2100 BC

[![Luna on the Parabiago plate (2nd–5th century), featuring the crescent crown, chariot and velificatio as lunar aspect found in different cultures.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Patera_di_Parabiago_-_MI_-_Museo_archeologico_-_Diana_-_Luna_-_25-7-2003_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_-_25-7-2003.jpg/250px-Patera_di_Parabiago_-_MI_-_Museo_archeologico_-_Diana_-_Luna_-_25-7-2003_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_-_25-7-2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patera_di_Parabiago_-_MI_-_Museo_archeologico_-_Diana_-_Luna_-_25-7-2003_-_Foto_Giovanni_Dall%27Orto_-_25-7-2003.jpg)

Crescent headgear, [chariot](/wiki/Luna_(goddess)#Chariot_of_the_Moon "Luna (goddess)") and [velificatio](/wiki/Velificatio "Velificatio") of [Luna](/wiki/Luna_(goddess) "Luna (goddess)"), 2nd–5th century

[![Rabbits are in a range of cultures identified with the Moon, from China to the Indigenous peoples of the Americas, as with the rabbit (on the left) of the Maya moon goddess (6th–9th century).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Goddess_O_Ixchel.jpg/250px-Goddess_O_Ixchel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goddess_O_Ixchel.jpg)

A [Moon rabbit](/wiki/Moon_rabbit "Moon rabbit") of the [Mayan moon goddess](/wiki/Maya_moon_goddess "Maya moon goddess"), 6th–9th century

Humans have not only observed the Moon since [prehistoric times](/wiki/Prehistoric_times "Prehistoric times"), but have also developed intricate perceptions of the Moon. Over time the Moon has been characterized and associated in many different ways, from having a [spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(vital_essence) "Spirit (vital essence)") or being [a deity](/wiki/Lunar_deity "Lunar deity"), and an [aspect](/wiki/Aspect_(religion) "Aspect (religion)") thereof or an aspect [in astrology](/wiki/Moon_(astrology) "Moon (astrology)"), being made an important part of many [cosmologies](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology").

This rich history of humans viewing the Moon has been evidenced starting with depictions from 40,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_present "Before present") and in written form from the [4th millennium BCE](/wiki/4th_millennium_BCE "4th millennium BCE") in the earliest cases of [writing](/wiki/Writing "Writing"). The oldest named astronomer and poet [Enheduanna](/wiki/Enheduanna "Enheduanna"), [Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_Empire "Akkadian Empire") high priestess to the lunar deity [Nanna/Sin](/wiki/Sin_(mythology) "Sin (mythology)") and daughter of [Sargon the Great](/wiki/Sargon_the_Great "Sargon the Great") (c. 2334 – c. 2279 BCE), tracked the Moon and wrote poems about her divine Moon.[[250]](#cite_note-c099-262)

#### Crescent
