For the representation of the Moon, especially its [lunar phases](/wiki/Lunar_phase "Lunar phase"), the [crescent](/wiki/Crescent "Crescent") (🌙) has been a recurring symbol in a range of cultures since at least 3,000 BCE or possibly earlier with bull horns dating to the earliest [cave paintings](/wiki/Cave_painting "Cave painting") at 40,000 [BP](/wiki/Before_present "Before present").[[245]](#cite_note-e093-257)[[252]](#cite_note-k874-264) In [writing systems](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") such as Chinese the crescent has developed into the symbol [月](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9C%88 "wikt:月"), the word for Moon, and in ancient Egyptian it was the symbol [𓇹](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%93%87%B9 "wikt:𓇹"), meaning Moon and spelled like the ancient Egyptian lunar deity *[Iah](/wiki/Iah "Iah")*,[[366]](#cite_note-Hart_2005_p._77-378) which the other ancient Egyptian lunar deities [Khonsu](/wiki/Khonsu "Khonsu") and [Thoth](/wiki/Thoth "Thoth") were associated with.

Iconographically the crescent was used in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia") as the primary symbol of [Nanna/Sîn](/wiki/Sin_(mythology) "Sin (mythology)"),[[249]](#cite_note-BlackGreen1992-261) the ancient [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumeria "Sumeria") lunar deity,[[367]](#cite_note-Nemet1998-379)[[249]](#cite_note-BlackGreen1992-261) who was the father of [Inanna/Ishtar](/wiki/Inanna "Inanna"), the goddess of the planet [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") (symbolized as the [eight pointed](/wiki/Octagram "Octagram") [Star of Ishtar](/wiki/Star_of_Ishtar "Star of Ishtar")),[[367]](#cite_note-Nemet1998-379)[[249]](#cite_note-BlackGreen1992-261) and [Utu/Shamash](/wiki/Utu "Utu"), the god of the Sun ([symbolized as a disc, optionally with eight rays](/wiki/Solar_symbol "Solar symbol")),[[367]](#cite_note-Nemet1998-379)[[249]](#cite_note-BlackGreen1992-261) all three often depicted next to each other. Nanna/Sîn is, like some other lunar deities, for example Iah and Khonsu of ancient Egypt, [Mene](/wiki/Mene_(goddess) "Mene (goddess)")/[Selene](/wiki/Selene "Selene") of ancient Greece and [Luna](/wiki/Luna_(mythology) "Luna (mythology)") of ancient Rome, depicted as a [horned deity](/wiki/Horned_deity "Horned deity"), featuring crescent shaped headgears or crowns.[[368]](#cite_note-380)[[369]](#cite_note-381)

The particular arrangement of the crescent with a star known as the [star and crescent](/wiki/Star_and_crescent "Star and crescent") (☪️) goes back to the Bronze Age, representing either the Sun and Moon, or the Moon and the planet Venus, in combination. It came to represent the selene goddess [Artemis](/wiki/Artemis "Artemis"), and via the patronage of [Hecate](/wiki/Hecate "Hecate"), which as [triple deity](/wiki/Triple_deity "Triple deity") under the [epithet](/wiki/Epithet "Epithet") *trimorphos*/*trivia* included aspects of Artemis/[Diana](/wiki/Diana_(mythology) "Diana (mythology)"), came to be used as a [symbol of Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantine_flags_and_insignia "Byzantine flags and insignia"), with [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") ([Queen of Heaven](/wiki/Queen_of_Heaven "Queen of Heaven")) later taking her place, becoming depicted in [Marian veneration](/wiki/Marian_veneration "Marian veneration") on a crescent and adorned with stars. Since then the [heraldric](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry") use of the star and crescent proliferated, Byzantium's symbolism possibly influencing the development of the [Ottoman flag](/wiki/Ottoman_flag "Ottoman flag"), specifically the combination of the Turkish crescent with a star,[[370]](#cite_note-382) and becoming a popular [symbol for Islam](/wiki/Symbols_of_Islam "Symbols of Islam") (as the *[hilal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/hilal#Noun "wikt:hilal")* of the [Islamic calendar](/wiki/Islamic_calendar "Islamic calendar")) and [for a range of nations](/wiki/Star_and_crescent#Contemporary_use "Star and crescent").[[371]](#cite_note-Kadoi_2014-383)

#### Other associations

The features of the Moon, the contrasting brighter highlands and darker maria, have been seen by different cultures [forming abstract shapes](/wiki/Lunar_pareidolia "Lunar pareidolia"). Such shapes are among others the [Man in the Moon](/wiki/Man_in_the_Moon "Man in the Moon") (e.g. [Coyolxāuhqui](/wiki/Coyolx%C4%81uhqui "Coyolxāuhqui")) or the [Moon Rabbit](/wiki/Moon_Rabbit "Moon Rabbit") (e.g. the Chinese [Tu'er Ye](/wiki/Tu%27er_Ye "Tu'er Ye") or in [Indigenous American mythologies](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the indigenous peoples of the Americas") the aspect of the [Mayan Moon goddess](/wiki/Maya_moon_goddess "Maya moon goddess"), from which possibly [Awilix](/wiki/Awilix "Awilix") is derived, or of [Metztli](/wiki/Metztli "Metztli")/[Tēcciztēcatl](/wiki/T%C4%93ccizt%C4%93catl "Tēcciztēcatl")).[[365]](#cite_note-Collections_Search_–_Museum_of_Fine_Arts,_Boston_1987-377)

Occasionally some lunar deities have been also depicted [driving a chariot across the sky](/wiki/Luna_(goddess)#Chariot_of_the_Moon "Luna (goddess)"), such as the Hindu [Chandra/Soma](/wiki/Chandra "Chandra"), the Greek Artemis, which is associated with Selene, or Luna, Selene's ancient Roman equivalent.

Color and material wise the Moon has been associated in Western [alchemy](/wiki/Alchemy "Alchemy") with [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), while gold is associated with the Sun.[[372]](#cite_note-Abbri_2019_pp._39–44-384)

Through a miracle, the so-called [splitting of the Moon](/wiki/Splitting_of_the_Moon "Splitting of the Moon") ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): انشقاق القمر) in [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), association with the Moon applies also to [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad").[[373]](#cite_note-385)

### Representation in modern culture

See also: [Moon in science fiction](/wiki/Moon_in_science_fiction "Moon in science fiction") and [List of appearances of the Moon in fiction](/wiki/List_of_appearances_of_the_Moon_in_fiction "List of appearances of the Moon in fiction")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Van_Gogh_-_Starry_Night_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Van_Gogh_-_Starry_Night_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Van_Gogh_-_Starry_Night_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

The Moon is prominently featured in [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh")'s 1889 painting *[The Starry Night](/wiki/The_Starry_Night "The Starry Night")*.[[374]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2019-386)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Melies_color_Voyage_dans_la_lune.jpg/250px-Melies_color_Voyage_dans_la_lune.jpg)](/wiki/File:Melies_color_Voyage_dans_la_lune.jpg)

An iconic image of the [Man in the Moon](/wiki/Man_in_the_Moon "Man in the Moon") from the first [science-fiction film](/wiki/Science-fiction_film "Science-fiction film") set in space, *[A Trip to the Moon](/wiki/A_Trip_to_the_Moon "A Trip to the Moon")* (1902, [Georges Méliès](/wiki/Georges_M%C3%A9li%C3%A8s "Georges Méliès")), inspired by a history of literature about going to the Moon.

The perception of the Moon in the [modern era](/wiki/Modern_era "Modern era") has been informed by [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope")-enabled [modern astronomy](/wiki/Modern_astronomy "Modern astronomy") and later by [spaceflight](/wiki/Spaceflight "Spaceflight") which enabled actual human activity at the Moon, particularly the [culturally impactful lunar landings](/wiki/Apollo_program#Cultural_impact "Apollo program"). These new insights inspired cultural references, connecting romantic reflections about the Moon[[375]](#cite_note-The_MIT_Press_Reader_2020-387) and speculative fiction such as science-fiction dealing with the Moon.[[374]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2019-386)[[376]](#cite_note-Nature_2019-388)

Contemporarily the Moon has been seen as a place for [economic expansion into space](/wiki/Commercialization_of_space "Commercialization of space"), with missions prospecting for [lunar resources](/wiki/Lunar_resources "Lunar resources"). This has been accompanied with renewed public and critical reflection on humanity's cultural and [legal relation to the celestial body](#Legal_status), especially regarding [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"),[[315]](#cite_note-Alvarez_2020_p.-327) as in the 1970 poem "[Whitey on the Moon](/wiki/Whitey_on_the_Moon "Whitey on the Moon")". In this light the Moon's nature has been invoked,[[348]](#cite_note-Declaration-360) particularly for lunar conservation[[317]](#cite_note-Nast_2013-329) and as a [common](/wiki/Commons "Commons").[[377]](#cite_note-Open_Lunar_Foundation_2022-389)[[342]](#cite_note-The_Space_Review_2021-354)[[350]](#cite_note-Evans_2021-362)

In 2021 20 July, the date of the [first crewed Moon landing](/wiki/Apollo_11#Landing "Apollo 11"), became the annual *International Moon Day*.[[378]](#cite_note-Nations_1967_a849-390)

### Lunar effect

Main article: [Lunar effect](/wiki/Lunar_effect "Lunar effect")

The lunar effect is an unproven correlation between specific stages of the roughly 29.5-day lunar cycle and behavior and physiological changes in living beings on Earth, including humans. The Moon has long been associated with insanity and irrationality; the words *lunacy* and *[lunatic](/wiki/Lunatic "Lunatic")* are derived from the Latin name for the Moon, *Luna*. Philosophers [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") and [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") argued that the full moon induced insanity in susceptible individuals, believing that the brain, which is mostly water, must be affected by the Moon and its power over the tides, but the Moon's gravity is too slight to affect any single person.[[379]](#cite_note-sciam-391) Even today, people who believe in a lunar effect claim that admissions to psychiatric hospitals, traffic accidents, homicides or suicides increase during a full moon,[[379]](#cite_note-sciam-391) but over 37 studies invalidate these claims.[[380]](#cite_note-RottonKelly1985-392)
Lunar cycles have significant impacts on human culture but no solid evidence connects these cycles to human biology.[[381]](#cite_note-FosterRoenneberg2008-393)

## Explanatory notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-inclination_4-0)** Between 18.29° and 28.58° to Earth's [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator")[[1]](#cite_note-W06-1)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-near-Earth_asteroids_5-0)** There are a number of [near-Earth asteroids](/wiki/Near-Earth_asteroid "Near-Earth asteroid"), including [3753 Cruithne](/wiki/3753_Cruithne "3753 Cruithne"), that are [co-orbital](/wiki/Co-orbital "Co-orbital") with Earth: their orbits bring them close to Earth for periods of time but then alter in the long term (Morais et al, 2002). These are [quasi-satellites](/wiki/Quasi-satellite "Quasi-satellite") – they are not moons as they do not orbit Earth. For more information, see [Other moons of Earth](/wiki/Other_moons_of_Earth "Other moons of Earth").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-maxval_18-0)** The *maximum value* is given based on scaling of the brightness from the value of −12.74 given for an equator to Moon-centre distance of 378 000 km in the NASA factsheet reference to the minimum Earth–Moon distance given there, after the latter is corrected for Earth's equatorial radius of 6 378 km, giving 350 600 km. The *minimum value* (for a distant [new moon](/wiki/New_moon "New moon")) is based on a similar scaling using the maximum Earth–Moon distance of 407 000 km (given in the factsheet) and by calculating the brightness of the [earthshine](/wiki/Earthshine "Earthshine") onto such a new moon. The brightness of the earthshine is [ Earth [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo") × ([Earth radius](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius") / Radius of [Moon's orbit](/wiki/Moon%27s_orbit "Moon's orbit"))2 ] relative to the direct solar illumination that occurs for a full moon. (Earth albedo = 0.367; Earth radius = (polar radius × equatorial radius)½ = 6 367 km.)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-angular_size_20-0)** The range of angular size values given are based on simple scaling of the following values given in the fact sheet reference: at an Earth-equator to Moon-centre distance of 378 000 km, the [angular size](/wiki/Angular_size "Angular size") is 1896 [arcseconds](/wiki/Arcsecond "Arcsecond"). The same fact sheet gives extreme Earth–Moon distances of 407 000 km and 357 000 km. For the maximum angular size, the minimum distance has to be corrected for Earth's equatorial radius of 6 378 km, giving 350 600 km.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-pressure_explanation_22-0)** Lucey et al. (2006) give 107 particles cm−3 by day and 105 particles cm−3 by night. Along with equatorial surface temperatures of 390 [K](/wiki/Kelvin "Kelvin") by day and 100 K by night, the [ideal gas law](/wiki/Ideal_gas_law "Ideal gas law") yields the pressures given in the infobox (rounded to the nearest [order of magnitude](/wiki/Order_of_magnitude "Order of magnitude")): 10−7 [Pa](/wiki/Pascal_(unit) "Pascal (unit)") by day and 10−10 Pa by night.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** Because the Moon follows an elliptical path, its distance from Earth varies over the year, ranging from 357,000–407,000 kilometres (222,000–253,000 mi).[[2]](#cite_note-NASAfactsheet-2)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** There is no strong correlation between the sizes of planets and the sizes of their satellites. Larger planets tend to have more satellites, both large and small, than smaller planets.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-Moon_vs._Charon_79-0)** With 27% the diameter and 60% the density of Earth, the Moon has 1.23% of the mass of Earth. The moon [Charon](/wiki/Charon_(moon) "Charon (moon)") is larger relative to its primary [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto"), but Earth and the Moon are different since Pluto is considered a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") and not a planet, unlike Earth.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-orbpd_195-0)** More accurately, the Moon's mean sidereal period (fixed star to fixed star) is 27.321661 days (27 d 07 h 43 min 11.5 s), and its mean tropical orbital period (from equinox to equinox) is 27.321582 days (27 d 07 h 43 min 04.7 s) (*Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Ephemeris*, 1961, at p.107).
10. **[^](#cite_ref-synpd_196-0)** More accurately, the Moon's mean synodic period (between mean solar conjunctions) is 29.530589 days (29 d 12 h 44 min 02.9 s) (*Explanatory Supplement to the Astronomical Ephemeris*, 1961, at p.107).
11. **[^](#cite_ref-size_changes_248-0)** See graph in [Sun#Life phases](/wiki/Sun#Life_phases "Sun"). At present, the diameter of the Sun is increasing at a rate of about five percent per billion years. This is very similar to the rate at which the apparent angular diameter of the Moon is decreasing as it recedes from Earth.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-area_252-0)** On average, the Moon covers an area of 0.21078 square degrees on the night sky.

370. **[^](#cite_ref-382)** "It seems possible, though not certain, that after the conquest Mehmed took over the crescent *and* star as an emblem of sovereignty from the Byzantines. The half-moon alone on a blood red flag, allegedly conferred on the Janissaries by Emir Orhan, was much older, as is demonstrated by numerous references to it dating from before 1453. But since these flags lack the star, which along with the half-moon is to be found on Sassanid and Byzantine municipal coins, it may be regarded as an innovation of Mehmed. It seems certain that in the interior of Asia tribes of Turkish nomads had been using the half-moon alone as an emblem for some time past, but it is equally certain that crescent and star *together* are attested only for a much later period. There is good reason to believe that old Turkish and Byzantine traditions were combined in the emblem of Ottoman and, much later, present-day Republican Turkish sovereignty." Franz Babinger (William C. Hickman Ed., Ralph Manheim Trans.), *Mehmed the Conqueror and His Time*, Princeton University Press, 1992, p 108
